other_observers: Nathan Pieplow; found and identified, after much effort, by both of us
description: the bird was a large Plover, about the same size as a Killdeer (one of the few shorebirds nearby). The bill was black, fairly short (for a shorebird), and thin but blunt-tipped. The legs were fairly long, and black.  The bird had a bold white eye-line, a darker cap with some gold-spangling, and a fairly dark (not white) face. The back was dark gray/brown, with bright gold spangles visible at close range. The underparts were mottled gray. In flight, the underwing was plain grayish-brown, with no black. The tail and rump were the same grayish-brown as the back, with some gold spangles. The flight feathers were blackish.  The wingtips were fairly long, with four primaries visible sticking out beyond the tertials.Behaviors: the bird was foraging on the shore of the lake, and was fairly tame. When pressed, it would fly to the opposite side of the lake.Call: When flushed the bird was heard to give a sorrowful sounding "chweedle" call, fairly high pitched and fast. When I listened to the call of American Golden-Plover immediately after the observation it was a perfect match.Plumage: juvenal
